story:
A nearly empty Italian restaurant, a warm summer night, and an outdoor table set the scene. Zoe loves the romance of eating under the sky, the way the moon softens edges and the air raises tiny goosebumps along her arm. Ben notices. His hand slides from her elbow to her wrist, gathering the chill, leaving heat. He meets her eyes—green, unreadable—and everything slows.

She leans closer, crosses her legs so they brush his, the black sundress riding a little higher. He tries for composure, sips bourbon, but his focus keeps drifting back to her. Their hands and words circle each other like a dance. He rests a hand on her leg without quite meaning to. They talk anyway, voices steady while the space between them sparks.

“How long will you be out of town?” she asks.

“Three days. Early flight Tuesday, meetings through Wednesday, travel back Thursday.” Disappointment flickers across her face. His hand squeezes, then inches higher, then pauses. The tablecloth shifts. He thinks about what it would mean to push past this moment, and doesn’t. Not yet.

She uncrosses her legs, lets him exist in that charged space, and keeps her tone even. “Chicago, right? Will you see the city at all?”

“Probably not. Dinners with clients.” He tries to be casual. “What about your week?”

“Slow. Only two shifts. They’re cutting hours.” She shrugs it off. “But it’s fine. I promised Jess I’d help with the baby. She gets overwhelmed sometimes. She always has.” Zoe smiles like this is just who she is: the steady friend.

“You’re a great friend,” he says, meaning it, and their faces tip nearer. A kiss hovers, unchosen.

A drop of condensation slips from her wineglass and lands at her collarbone, tracing a cool path downward. Ben’s eyes follow. His fingertip does too, stopping at the line of her dress. Her breath catches and releases.

“You dripped a bit,” he whispers.

“The night air,” she says, breathy but teasing. “It makes everything feel warmer.”

They ride the current—legs touching, hearts racing, each shift of skin a question. He reaches for the chilled bottle. “Can I fill you up?”

“Yes,” she says, a little too quickly, then tempers it with a smile. She takes a cube of ice and rolls it over her lips, along her wrist, across the flushed skin of her chest, letting the meltwater run. She watches him watch her. He shifts in his chair, the moment pressing in from all sides.

It was getting uncomfortable.

“Sorry that took so long! GiGi was a mess, wouldn’t let me change her!” Jess returns in a rush of relief and noise, kisses Ben’s cheek, collapses into her seat, and passes the baby over. Ben kisses the tiny head, soft hair standing. The spell cracks, daylight cutting through.

“No problem,” Zoe says, smile fixed in place. “Ben was telling me about his trip. Perks of marrying a successful salesman—he’s always on the move.”

“I knew what I was signing up for,” Jess laughs, already sipping her soda too fast. “Good thing I have you when he’s gone. Thanks for coming tonight. Ben keeps saying you should come out with us more.”

“Girls’ night,” Zoe replies lightly. “We’ll make it fun.”

Ben hands GiGi back as Jess pulls out a bottle and a burp cloth. He risks a glance at Zoe. She risks one back.

It was getting uncomfortable.
